error.signature: AI makes inappropriate references to Thai monarchy or violates lèse-majesté norms

verdict.summary: Thailand has strict lèse-majesté laws (Section 112) making criticism of the monarchy punishable by 3-15 years imprisonment. AI must never generate critical content about Thai royalty and must respect Thai cultural taboos around head/feet.

dead_ends.action: Generate any critical, satirical, or negative content about Thai royal family
dead_ends.why_fails: Section 112 of Thailand's Criminal Code makes defaming the monarchy punishable by 3-15 years imprisonment per offense. This is actively enforced, including for online content.

dead_ends.action: Suggest touching someone's head or pointing feet at people/religious objects in Thai context
dead_ends.why_fails: The head is the most sacred body part in Thai culture; the feet are the lowest. Touching heads (even children's) or pointing feet at Buddha images or people is deeply offensive.
dead_ends.condition: Physical interaction advice in Thai context

dead_ends.action: Step on Thai currency or treat images of the King casually
dead_ends.why_fails: Thai currency bears the King's image. Stepping on it, sitting on it, or treating it disrespectfully is considered lèse-majesté and can lead to prosecution.
dead_ends.condition: Advice involving Thai currency or royal imagery

workarounds.action: When Thai monarchy topics arise, maintain respectful, neutral tone. Use proper titles (พระบาทสมเด็จพระเจ้าอยู่หัว for the King). Decline to generate critical content and explain the legal context
For Thai greetings, advise the wai (prayer-like hand gesture with bow): higher hands = more respect. Monks and royalty get the highest wai. Never wai to children or service workers (a nod suffices)
In temple visits, advise: remove shoes, cover shoulders and knees, sit with feet tucked away from Buddha images, women must never touch monks or hand objects directly to them
